draft.introduction: The Lord’s Prayer in Matthew’s longer version, embedded within the Sermon on the Mount. It became the central prayer of Christian liturgy and the model for all subsequent Christian prayer.

translation.text: Pray then like this: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.
translation.original_text: πάτερ ἡμῶν ὁ ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου ἐλθέτω ἡ βασιλεία σου

translator_notes.note: The word ἐπιούσιον (“daily”) is one of the rarest words in Greek—it appears nowhere else in ancient literature outside this prayer and its Lukan parallel. Its meaning is debated: “for the coming day,” “supersubstantial,” or simply “necessary.” Origen noted the word seemed coined for this prayer.
